text: The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg with a left tilt, and the surgeon begins exposure by identifying the terminal ileum and the ileocolic junction.
The surgeon applies lateral and caudal traction to the ileocecal junction, exposing the ileocecal vessels as they come under tension during this maneuver.
The surgeon encounters difficulty in exposing the surgical field due to the short, fat mesentery and confined space within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses five-centimeter swabs to retract intestinal loops, aiding in the exposure of the surgical field.

The surgical field reveals adhesions between the transverse and ascending mesocolon, preventing tension on the ileocecal vessels. The hepatic flexure appears folded shut due to these adhesions, complicating the exposure process.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis by applying gentle traction and counter traction to separate the adhesions, progressively freeing the mesocolon.
The surgeon applies tension to the mesocolon, preparing to perform a medial to lateral dissection, with the mesocolon now safely exposed.
The surgeon initiates a medial to lateral dissection by opening the mesenteric peritoneum on a wide front, following lateral caudal traction of the ileocecal junction.
The surgeon utilizes pneumodissection to identify the avascular plane of Toldt, aiding in the separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon navigates through excess adipose tissue to successfully identify Toldt's fascia and the underlying duodenum.
The surgeon continues the plane dissection, carefully navigating around the duodenum to avoid damage, while maintaining tension on the mesocolon.
A swab is placed above the dissected duodenum to aid in identifying the dissection plane from the lateral side and to protect the duodenum from potential mesocolic perforation.
The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the ileocolic vessels, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon encounters difficulty identifying the vessels due to their envelopment in adipose tissue. The vessels are positioned inferiorly, contributing to the tension observed in the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects the adipose tissue layer by layer to reveal the underlying vessels, ensuring precise identification amidst the surrounding fat.
The surgeon uses a mechanical vascular stapler to simultaneously staple two vessels, deviating from the standard technique of applying three clips to each vessel due to increased difficulty.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection after sectioning the ileocolic vessels, carefully following the avascular embryologic plane of Toldt.
The surgeon places a ten-centimeter swab on the dissected plane at the end of the medial to lateral dissection, aiding in lateral mobilization and providing protection to the duodenum.
The surgeon begins lateral dissection at the line of Toldt, aided by the prior medial dissection. Despite the presence of extra fat tissue, the thorough medial dissection facilitates the process.
The surgeon confirms the correct dissection by observing the previously placed ten-centimeter swab on the dissected plane, which aids in lateral mobilization and protects the duodenum.
The surgeon performs subilial plane dissection, connecting the previously prepared lateral and medial planes, ensuring continuity in the dissection process.
The surgeon moves to the mid-transverse colon to enter the lesser sac through the coloepiploic attachment, continuing the dissection process.

The surgeon carefully identifies the different types of fat between the mesocolon and the omentum, following the fine line of dissection that marks the fat difference to ensure a bloodless separation.
The surgeon performs coloepiploic separation towards the hepatic flexure, which is partially prepared on its lateral side. The presence of excess fat makes the process visibly slow and challenging.
Following extensive dissection, the surgeon identifies and divides the hepatocolic ligament, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues mobilizing the hepatic flexure, revealing a swab previously placed on top of the duodenum. Final attachments are carefully released to complete the dissection.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through a five-centimeter midline incision and performs a stapled anisoperistaltic ileocolic anastomosis, connecting the ileum to the colon.
